The most significant issue is the lack of formal theoretical grounding for why manifold distance should reliably separate adversarial from legitimate samples, and the empirical evaluation appears incomplete without details on dataset splits, hyperparameters, or comparison to a wider range of modern defenses. The manuscript requires substantial revision to meet the standards of a top-tier venue.

comments.title: Inconsistent mention of graph attacks in an image classification paper
comments.quote: Attackers can deceive the DNNs model by manipulating graph structure or node features, which results in graph counter disturbances
comments.explanation: The paper focuses on adversarial examples for image classification (ImageNet dataset) and only discusses image-based attacks (FGSM, BIM, DeepFool, etc.) in the related work. The sudden mention of 'graph structure or node features' and 'graph counter disturbances' is out of place and suggests a possible leftover from a different paper. This inconsistency could mislead readers about the scope of the work.

comments.title: Typo in attack name: 'Acobian' instead of 'Jacobian'
comments.quote: Acobian Saliency Map Approach (JSMA)
comments.explanation: The well-known attack is the Jacobian-based Saliency Map Approach (JSMA). 'Acobian' is almost certainly an OCR error for 'Jacobian'. While the acronym JSMA is correctly given, the misspelled name could cause confusion for readers unfamiliar with the attack.

comments.title: Non-standard notation for DI²-FGSM
comments.quote: Diverse Input Iterative Fast Gradient Sign Method (DI[2] - FGSM)
comments.explanation: The standard name is DI²-FGSM (Diverse Inputs Iterative Fast Gradient Sign Method), where the superscript 2 is often written as ^2. The bracket notation [2] and the space before the hyphen are likely OCR artifacts. Although the meaning is clear from context, the notation deviates from the usual convention.

comments.title: Confusing claim about adversarial training and generalization
comments.quote: But adversarial training diminishes the ML model’s accuracy and can make the ML model more exposed to generalization(Carmon et al., 2019).
comments.explanation: The phrase 'exposed to generalization' is unclear. It likely intends to say that adversarial training harms generalization (e.g., reduces test accuracy on benign samples) or leads to overfitting. The citation Carmon et al. (2019) actually shows that adversarial training can be improved to maintain generalization, so the cited source may not support the claim as stated. The context does not resolve the ambiguity, and a careful reader may misinterpret the intended effect.

comments.title: Mischaracterization of defensive distillation's vulnerability
comments.quote: The black-box attack’s recent improvement makes this out-of-date defense (Chakraborty et al., 2018). The strong transfer-potential of adversarial samples across neural network models (Papernot et al., 2016c) is the main reason for this method’s collapse.
comments.explanation: The text conflates two different concepts: black-box attacks and transferability. Defensive distillation was shown to be ineffective primarily because it provides a false sense of security against white-box attacks (as demonstrated by Carlini & Wagner, 2016), not because of black-box attack improvements. The cited Papernot et al. (2016c) is about transferability, but the paragraph's structure suggests that black-box attack improvements directly cause distillation's collapse, which is misleading. The explanation is incomplete and could mislead a reader about the actual failure mode of distillation.

comments.title: Unsubstantiated claim about ensemble defenses
comments.quote: (He et al., 2017) concluded that combining/ensemble weak defenses does not automatically improve a system’s robustness. Also, the ensemble technique remains static and vulnerable to a new attack.
comments.explanation: The citation He et al. (2017) is about adversarial training and robust optimization, not about ensembling weak preprocessing defenses. The paper actually shows that combining multiple robust models can improve robustness. The authors' claim that ensemble techniques are 'static and vulnerable to a new attack' is asserted without evidence and contradicts the main contribution of the paper (MDJ itself is an ensemble-like orchestration). This appears to be a self-contradictory or poorly referenced claim that undermines the paper's own methodology.

comments.title: Incomprehensible sentence about adversarial vs legitimate sample discrimination
comments.quote: For deep learning models, if the adversarial samples and legitimate samples usually do not appear in which the samples are discriminated in high-dimensional space, it can be found that they are closer to the manifold than the legitimate negative samples.
comments.explanation: The sentence is grammatically broken and semantically incoherent. It seems to attempt a statement about the relative distances of adversarial vs legitimate samples to a manifold, but the phrase 'do not appear in which the samples are discriminated' is nonsensical. The surrounding context (discussing binary classification and sparsity of legitimate samples) does not clarify the intended meaning. This is not just a typo; it prevents a careful reader from understanding a key theoretical point about manifold-based detection.

comments.title: Ambiguous claim about dimensionality and detectability
comments.quote: The classification model is easy to be attacked because the dimensionality of the manifold has been excessively reduced. Increasing the dimensionality of the manifold can distinguish adversarial samples, but it is difficult to solve the direction of increasing dimensionality.
comments.explanation: The claim that 'excessively reduced' manifold dimensionality causes vulnerability is vague and not supported by the manifold hypothesis literature. The standard view (e.g., from Goodfellow et al., 2014) is that adversarial examples exist because of local linearity, not reduced dimensionality. Moreover, 'increasing the dimensionality of the manifold' is not a standard concept — manifolds have fixed intrinsic dimension; you can change the embedding dimension, but that's different. The phrase 'direction of increasing dimensionality' is undefined. This could mislead a reader into thinking the authors propose a technical solution (dimension increase) that is not actually implemented in MDJ.

comments.title: Proposition A and B are not actually formal propositions
comments.quote: We can assume that the manifold is proposition A: then there is manifold M and algorithm A, if x is distributed as P(X), then x is on M. The defense detection uses its negative proposition B: that is, there are manifold M* and algorithm A*. If the distribution of x is not P(X), then x is not on M*.
comments.explanation: The text calls these 'propositions' but they are not stated as formal logical statements; they are imprecise and contain undefined symbols (M, A, M*, A*). The 'negative proposition B' is not the logical negation of A (which would be 'x is not on M'), but instead introduces new manifolds and algorithms. The notation and reasoning are too vague to constitute a proof or even a clear argument. A careful reader cannot determine what is being claimed or how it justifies the existence of adversarial detection methods.

comments.title: Incomplete sentence in defensive distillation analysis
comments.quote: But for unknown attacks, the sample
comments.explanation: The sentence ends abruptly after 'the sample' with no punctuation or continuation. The next line starts a new subsection on preprocessing methods. This leaves the reader unable to determine the authors' intended conclusion about defensive distillation for unknown attacks. The context does not resolve the missing content; it appears to be an editing or OCR artifact. The meaning is unclear.

comments.title: Unsubstantiated claim about optimal defense-attack correspondence
comments.quote: For each defense method, there should be a corresponding optimal defense attack method, and for each attack, there should be an optimal defense method.
comments.explanation: This claim is presented as a general principle without justification or citation. While it may be an intuitive motivation for the orchestration strategy, it is not a proven theoretical result in adversarial machine learning. The statement could mislead readers into thinking a rigorous duality exists, which is not established. The context (manifold analysis) does not provide proof. The claim overstates what has been shown.

comments.title: Underspecified 'Heuristics method'
comments.quote: Therefore, this paper uses the Heuristics method to learn the original high-dimensional sample space which changes in the low-dimensional manifold.
comments.explanation: The term 'Heuristics method' is capitalized but not defined, cited, or described. It is unclear whether it refers to a specific known technique (e.g., heuristic search, a named algorithm) or a custom procedure. The surrounding text does not clarify what this method entails, making the description ambiguous and non-reproducible. This is a key step in their approach to learning the manifold changes.

comments.title: Underspecified manifold distance detection method
comments.quote: We use a simple method, to reflect the transformation of the distance of the picture manifold. ... It is the basic idea of the manifold distance detection method.
comments.explanation: The passage attempts to describe the manifold distance detection method, but the description is critically vague. The 'simple method' is never defined; the text only mentions using the softmax output f(x) and then cautions that using only a single probability is not significant. This leaves a gap: the method must rely on more than a single probability (e.g., probability divergence across classes or after transformations), but that core mechanism is omitted. Without specifying what quantity is computed (threshold, divergence measure, etc.) and how it is used to decide whether a sample is adversarial, a reader cannot understand or reproduce the detection approach. The earlier summary mentions a 'probability divergence detector', but this passage does not incorporate any divergence concept, creating further ambiguity. The statement 'It is the basic idea' suggests the explanation is complete, yet the actual operational procedure remains unclear.

comments.title: Overclaim about detecting all CW samples
comments.quote: especially on CW attacks, we can detect all pairs of samples
comments.explanation: The text claims that all pairs of CW attack samples are detected, but Table 5 shows a recall of 0.99 for CWL_i (Next), not 1.00. While the LL entries are 1.00, the Next entry for CWL_i is 0.99, so not all pairs. This is a minor overstatement.

comments.title: Overclaim that orchestration strategy is better than other combined defense methods
comments.quote: The experimental effect proves that our orchestration defense strategy is better than other combined defense methods
comments.explanation: The experimental comparison is only against Feature Squeezing, not against other combined defense methods. The phrase 'other combined defense methods' could be interpreted as referring to the Comb1 and Comb2 variants discussed earlier, but the sentence appears in the comparison section with Feature Squeezing, implying a broader claim. It overstates what has been demonstrated.

comments.title: Underspecified 'math method' and 'difference method'
comments.quote: the input transformation chose the Median Filter, and the math method selected the difference method instead of the maximum value
comments.explanation: The terms 'math method' and 'difference method' are not defined in the provided context. The paper earlier describes combining preprocessing methods but does not explain how 'math method' (possibly a method for combining outputs or computing distances) or 'difference method' (as opposed to maximum) are implemented. This omission makes it difficult for a reader to reproduce the optimal combination or understand the decision rule.

comments.title: Inconsistent abbreviation: 'Com3' vs 'Comb3'
comments.quote: Comb represent the Com3 in the previous Section4.
comments.explanation: The text uses 'Com3' instead of 'Comb3', which is used elsewhere (e.g., 'Comb3' in the preceding paragraph). This is a minor inconsistency that could confuse readers, though the intended meaning is clear.

comments.title: Missing word in experiment setup description
comments.quote: following the same experiment set-up d in Feature Squeezing paper
comments.explanation: The phrase 'set-up d' appears to be missing a word (likely 'as in' or 'described in'). This is likely an OCR artifact, but it makes the sentence grammatically incomplete and slightly ambiguous.

progressive__glm-4.7-flash.label: Progressive (glm-4.7-flash)
progressive__glm-4.7-flash.overall_feedback: This paper presents Manifold Distance Judge (MDJ), a defense strategy that leverages the manifold hypothesis to distinguish adversarial examples through an orchestrated image processing pipeline. While the motivation—defending existing DNNs without model modification—is compelling, the manuscript currently suffers from clarity issues, including awkward phrasing and a lack of formal technical definitions, particularly regarding the term "Service Orchestration" referenced in the title. A significant issue is the theoretical justification for why expanding manifold distance successfully detects complex attacks like CW and DI-FGSM; the text provides qualitative intuition rather than a formal mathematical treatment of the distance metrics involved. Furthermore, the choice of Feature Squeezing as a primary baseline comparison appears insufficient for ICLR standards, as the proposed orchestration strategy is intended to be superior to simple random combinations of defenses; the paper must provide rigorous experimental evidence and theoretical analysis to demonstrate the superiority of the specific orchestration over standard single-modality defenses.

comments.title: Inconsistent Domain Terminology in Introduction
comments.quote: Attackers can deceive the DNNs model by manipulating graph structure or node features, which results in graph counter disturbances
comments.explanation: The passage discusses the domain of image classification (specifically ImageNet in the abstract and 'image processing' in later sections) and standard image-based attacks (FGSM, CW, etc.). However, the Introduction abruptly introduces 'graph structure' and 'node features,' which are terminology typically associated with Graph Neural Networks (GNNs) rather than Convolutional Neural Networks (CNNs) for image tasks. This creates a logical contradiction, suggesting the authors may have conflated literature from GNNs with their image-based work. Furthermore, 'graph counter disturbances' is a non-standard and confusing phrase. This could mislead the reader into thinking the paper covers graph data, whereas it focuses on image data.

comments.title: Misformatted Superscript in Related Work
comments.quote: Diverse Input Iterative Fast Gradient Sign Method (DI[2] - FGSM)
comments.explanation: The notation DI[2] is technically incorrect and likely an OCR artifact. The standard notation for the 'Diverse Inputs Iterative FGSM' proposed by Xie et al. (2019) uses a superscript, written as DI^2-FGSM or DI²-FGSM. The literal square bracket '[2]' confuses the reader and breaks standard typographical conventions for denoting iteration powers or mathematical exponents.

comments.title: Contradictory Explanation of Misclassification Reasons
comments.quote: From the perspective of two attack optimization models, there are two reasons for a classifier misclassifies an adversarial example: (1) The adversarial example is far from the boundary of the manifold of the task. (2) The adversarial example is close to the boundary of the manifold.
comments.explanation: The text attempts to explain why a classifier fails to correctly classify adversarial samples based on their position relative to a boundary. However, the two reasons listed are contradictory: misclassification typically requires the sample to cross the decision boundary, implying it must be 'close' to the boundary. Being 'far from the boundary' would usually indicate the classifier is confident and correct. The logic breaks down; the authors seem to conflate 'far from the normative training manifold' (an outlier) with being 'far from the boundary', or they fail to distinguish between the geometry of the manifold and the decision boundary. This makes it difficult for a reader to understand the theoretical basis for the proposed detection method.

comments.title: Inconsistent Notation in Dimension Description
comments.quote: take images as an example, MxNx8 black and white images
comments.explanation: The passage describes a set of images as 'MxNx8 black and white images'. Notation MxNx8 typically implies a 3-dimensional tensor (Height x Width x Channels), which corresponds to a multi-channel image (e.g., RGB is usually 3 channels). However, the descriptor 'black and white' contradicts this, as black and white (grayscale) images typically have only a single channel (e.g., MxN). While it is possible this refers to a specific paper with non-standard dimensions or a corrupted file format, in standard academic writing, 'MxNx8' is inconsistent with 'black and white'. It is likely an OCR error where 'MxNx3' (for RGB) was misread as '8', or the '1' channel size was misread.

comments.title: Imprecise Description of Adversarial Training Mechanism
comments.quote: The defense method is to superimpose the disturbance that maximizes the loss function in the sample, so it is effective for fixed or large disturbances...
comments.explanation: The sentence describes the defense mechanism as 'superimposing the disturbance that maximizes the loss function'. While this is a step involved in adversarial *generation*, it is incomplete for describing *Adversarial Training* as a defense. The defense mechanism is actually 'training the model on samples that have been perturbed/adversarially generated'. The phrasing omits the critical step of model retraining/learning, making the description vague. Furthermore, the subsequent clause 'even if the attack method with obvious changes in the model gradient is easy to be detected' suggests a logical connection that is hard to parse. Adversarial training is robust to the attack itself, not necessarily robust to the *detectability* of the attack method.

comments.title: Inconsistent Terminology: 'Service Orchestration'
comments.quote: We propose a new idea of service orchestration strategy to combine different methods to expand the manifold distance.
comments.explanation: In the context of image classification and defense, 'Service Orchestration' typically refers to coordinating software services or microservices (e.g., in cloud computing), not to combining image preprocessing methods. The passage describes simply mixing preprocessing techniques like Random Zoom, Swirl, and Median Filter. This strongly suggests the authors intended to say 'Sample Orchestration' or 'Defense Orchestration' (or possibly 'Ensemble'). While the title of the paper uses 'Service Orchestration', the definition provided in this paragraph is logically inconsistent with the content of the method, leading to potential confusion.

comments.title: Incorrect Terminology: 'Cropped' for Padding
comments.quote: where the distance from each edge of the image is cropped by U[10, 50]
comments.explanation: In image processing, 'cropping' generally means removing a portion of the image (decreasing the size), whereas the context describes adding a margin to each edge (padding) to effectively zoom in on a central portion of the image. You cannot 'crop a distance'. The phrase 'cropped by' is a direct contradiction to the geometric operation intended (adding pixels to the boundary). It should be 'padded by' or 'cropped from'.

comments.title: Undefined Term: 'Position Distance'
comments.quote: To indicate the change of position distance we propose a manifold distance detection method
comments.explanation: The passage introduces the concept of a 'position distance' without definition. The method described subsequently utilizes the 'output probability of softmax', which is a scalar confidence score, not a geometric position or 'position distance'. Given the paper's theme is 'Manifold Distance Judge', it is highly probable that 'position' is a typo for 'manifold', as 'change of manifold distance' is a coherent concept in this context.

comments.title: Ambiguous Description of 'Random Zoom'
comments.quote: distance from each edge of the image is cropped by U[10, 50], independently for each edge.
comments.explanation: Similar to the 'cropped' issue above, the description 'cropped by U[10, 50]' is physically impossible. A distance cannot be 'cropped'. It implies the author meant to describe a padding operation (adding pixels to the edges) which is standard in Random Zoom/Crop defenses. The lack of precise terminology makes the method slightly underspecified for a reproduction attempt.

comments.title: Missing mathematical formula (OCR artifact)
comments.quote:
We use a simple method, 

 to reflect the transformation of the distance of the picture manifold.
comments.explanation: There is a clear formatting gap between the phrase 'We use a simple method,' and 'to reflect...' in the provided OCR text. In academic writing, this structure typically precedes a formula or a mathematical definition (e.g., a divergence measure or difference in logits). The absence of the explicit mathematical definition here renders the 'simple method' underspecified, which is critical as this method is the core logic for the 'basic idea of the manifold distance detection method'. Even if the definition exists elsewhere in the paper, its omission here hinders the immediate understanding of the proposed technique.

comments.title: Logical contradiction regarding 'spatial mapping'
comments.quote: Using only a single probability means that the effect is not significant for adversarial attacks, especially for adversarial samples that have greatly crossed the boundary, their spatial mapping is almost the same as that of legitimate samples.
comments.explanation: The text claims that adversarial samples 'have greatly crossed the boundary' and yet 'their spatial mapping is almost the same as that of legitimate samples.' This contradicts the premise established in the preceding sentence, where 'the size of the probability output represents the shape of the input x in the low-dimensional manifold mapping'. If a sample 'greatly crosses the boundary' of the manifold (changing its classification), its representation within the low-dimensional manifold mapping must differ significantly from the legitimate samples (which occupy specific local regions of the manifold). The statement implies the mapping is identical, which undermines the theoretical justification for distinguishing them via manifold distance. It is likely the authors meant 'spatial mapping in the raw pixel space' is the same, but this was not stated.

comments.title: Inconsistency between Rank-based and Probability-based definitions
comments.quote: Using only a single probability means that the effect is not significant...
comments.explanation: The text proposes using 'only a single probability' as the basis for detection. However, the 'Key Equations' and 'Theorems' in the provided summary define the 'Attack Success Condition' based on the 'Rank function' (i.e., rank(L,1) != rank(L2,1)). While a high probability for a single class correlates with a high rank, relying on a single probability threshold is a different heuristic from a rank-based metric. The text does not reconcile this distinction, leading to potential ambiguity about exactly which statistic constitutes the 'manifold distance detection method'.

comments.title: Undefined table header notation
comments.quote: Table 5: Contrast with Feature Squeezing ... |_Attack_| |_s_| |_Recall_|
comments.explanation: The table header includes the symbol \_s_\_ (or 's') without any definition in the surrounding text or the paper's summary. While this appears to be a specific condition or subset label (e.g., 'Legitimate' vs 'Adversarial' or 'Step'), its meaning remains opaque to a reader without access to the original paper or definition elsewhere in the text. This constitutes an inconsistent or insufficiently defined notation for a table intended to be self-explanatory.

comments.title: Ambiguous sampling terminology
comments.quote: especially on CW attacks, we can detect all pairs of samples
comments.explanation: The phrase 'all pairs of samples' is linguistically awkward in this context. Since the metric reported is 'Recall' (suggesting True Positive Rate), the authors mean they detected all adversarial samples (or all samples correctly). 'Pairs of samples' could imply (sample, label) pairs, but this is non-standard terminology in adversarial detection metrics. A reader might reasonably be confused about whether this refers to sample pairs, instance pairs, or image-label pairs.

comments.title: Misleading terminology for table values
comments.quote: the best classification rate of feature squeezing is only 0.87
comments.explanation: The text compares 'Recall value' (the column header in Table 5) of MDJ to the 'classification rate' of Feature Squeezing. In adversarial defense literature, 'classification rate' (or accuracy) typically refers to the benign accuracy or overall classification performance (TP + TN), whereas 'Recall' refers to True Positive Rate (detection rate). Calling the table's Recall values 'classification rate' conflates detection performance with overall classification accuracy, which could mislead a reader about the trade-off involved.

comments.title: Typographical error in experiment setup description
comments.quote: following the same experiment set-up d in Feature Squeezing paper
comments.explanation: The phrase 'experiment set-up d' is grammatically and technically incorrect. 'd' appears to be an OCR artifact or a typo. It likely meant 'set-up as', 'setup', or 'set-up defined'. The extra character 'd' is nonsensical in this context and disrupts the flow of the sentence.

comments.title: Grammatical error in table label definition
comments.quote: Comb represent the Com3 in the previous Section4
comments.explanation: The sentence uses the singular verb 'represent' with the plural subject 'Comb'. It should read 'Comb3 represents' or 'Comb represents the Com3 strategy'. This is a minor grammatical inconsistency that could stem from OCR processing of the original manuscript.

comments.title: Notation inconsistency between $M$ and $M^*$
comments.quote: The defense detection uses its negative proposition B: that is, there are manifold M* and algorithm A*. If the distribution of x is not P(X), then x is not on M*.
comments.explanation: In the same paragraph, the text references Proposition A (which establishes the manifold $M$) and then Proposition B (which introduces a new symbol $M^*$ without explicit definition in this excerpt). While the intention is likely to define the manifold of the transformed or processed space, the abrupt introduction of $M^*$ creates ambiguity. If read as a typo for $M$, it implies adversarial samples are 'off the manifold,' which contradicts the standard view that adversarial examples exist in the same high-dimensional space as benign samples, though they may cross the decision boundary.

comments.title: Ambiguous phrasing for Random Zoom parameters
comments.quote: where the distance from each edge of the image is cropped by U[10, 50], independently for each edge.
comments.explanation: The phrase 'distance from each edge... is cropped by U[10, 50]' is linguistically awkward. In image processing, one usually 'crops by' an amount (e.g., crop by 10 pixels) or 'sets the margin to' a value. Reading this literally suggests the *distance* is the object being cropped, which is physically impossible. It is much more likely the authors meant 'cropping margins of size U[10, 50] from each edge,' but the current phrasing could confuse a reader regarding whether the U[10, 50] refers to the margin size or the crop extent (which is the same, but the 'distance' word muddles the logic).

comments.title: Ambiguous structure of Proposition A
comments.quote: We can assume that the manifold is proposition A: then there is manifold M and algorithm A, if x is distributed as P(X), then x is on M.
comments.explanation: The sentence structure 'Manifold is proposition A: then there is manifold M' is grammatically broken and logically circular. It appears to be a transcription error or a poorly phrased hypothesis. The content seems to say: 'Proposition A states: If x is distributed as P(X), then x is on manifold M.' While the mathematical content (valid data lives on the manifold) is consistent with the Manifold Hypothesis, the formatting of the proposition statement is insufficiently clear to a reviewer.

comments.title: Ambiguous Description of 'Random Zoom'
comments.quote: distance from each edge of the image is cropped by U[10, 50], independently for each edge.
comments.explanation: Similar to the 'cropped' issue above, the description 'cropped by U[10, 50]' is physically impossible. A distance cannot be 'cropped'. It implies the author meant to describe a padding operation (adding pixels to the edges) which is standard in Random Zoom/Crop defenses. The lack of precise terminology makes the method slightly underspecified for a reproduction attempt.

comments.title: Missing mathematical formula (OCR artifact)
comments.quote:
We use a simple method, 

 to reflect the transformation of the distance of the picture manifold.
comments.explanation: There is a clear formatting gap between the phrase 'We use a simple method,' and 'to reflect...' in the provided OCR text. In academic writing, this structure typically precedes a formula or a mathematical definition (e.g., a divergence measure or difference in logits). The absence of the explicit mathematical definition here renders the 'simple method' underspecified, which is critical as this method is the core logic for the 'basic idea of the manifold distance detection method'. Even if the definition exists elsewhere in the paper, its omission here hinders the immediate understanding of the proposed technique.

comments.title: Logical contradiction regarding 'spatial mapping'
comments.quote: Using only a single probability means that the effect is not significant for adversarial attacks, especially for adversarial samples that have greatly crossed the boundary, their spatial mapping is almost the same as that of legitimate samples.
comments.explanation: The text claims that adversarial samples 'have greatly crossed the boundary' and yet 'their spatial mapping is almost the same as that of legitimate samples.' This contradicts the premise established in the preceding sentence, where 'the size of the probability output represents the shape of the input x in the low-dimensional manifold mapping'. If a sample 'greatly crosses the boundary' of the manifold (changing its classification), its representation within the low-dimensional manifold mapping must differ significantly from the legitimate samples (which occupy specific local regions of the manifold). The statement implies the mapping is identical, which undermines the theoretical justification for distinguishing them via manifold distance. It is likely the authors meant 'spatial mapping in the raw pixel space' is the same, but this was not stated.

comments.title: Inconsistency between Rank-based and Probability-based definitions
comments.quote: Using only a single probability means that the effect is not significant...
comments.explanation: The text proposes using 'only a single probability' as the basis for detection. However, the 'Key Equations' and 'Theorems' in the provided summary define the 'Attack Success Condition' based on the 'Rank function' (i.e., rank(L,1) != rank(L2,1)). While a high probability for a single class correlates with a high rank, relying on a single probability threshold is a different heuristic from a rank-based metric. The text does not reconcile this distinction, leading to potential ambiguity about exactly which statistic constitutes the 'manifold distance detection method'.

comments.title: Undefined table header notation
comments.quote: Table 5: Contrast with Feature Squeezing ... |_Attack_| |_s_| |_Recall_|
comments.explanation: The table header includes the symbol \_s_\_ (or 's') without any definition in the surrounding text or the paper's summary. While this appears to be a specific condition or subset label (e.g., 'Legitimate' vs 'Adversarial' or 'Step'), its meaning remains opaque to a reader without access to the original paper or definition elsewhere in the text. This constitutes an inconsistent or insufficiently defined notation for a table intended to be self-explanatory.

comments.title: Ambiguous sampling terminology
comments.quote: especially on CW attacks, we can detect all pairs of samples
comments.explanation: The phrase 'all pairs of samples' is linguistically awkward in this context. Since the metric reported is 'Recall' (suggesting True Positive Rate), the authors mean they detected all adversarial samples (or all samples correctly). 'Pairs of samples' could imply (sample, label) pairs, but this is non-standard terminology in adversarial detection metrics. A reader might reasonably be confused about whether this refers to sample pairs, instance pairs, or image-label pairs.

comments.title: Misleading terminology for table values
comments.quote: the best classification rate of feature squeezing is only 0.87
comments.explanation: The text compares 'Recall value' (the column header in Table 5) of MDJ to the 'classification rate' of Feature Squeezing. In adversarial defense literature, 'classification rate' (or accuracy) typically refers to the benign accuracy or overall classification performance (TP + TN), whereas 'Recall' refers to True Positive Rate (detection rate). Calling the table's Recall values 'classification rate' conflates detection performance with overall classification accuracy, which could mislead a reader about the trade-off involved.

comments.title: Typographical error in experiment setup description
comments.quote: following the same experiment set-up d in Feature Squeezing paper
comments.explanation: The phrase 'experiment set-up d' is grammatically and technically incorrect. 'd' appears to be an OCR artifact or a typo. It likely meant 'set-up as', 'setup', or 'set-up defined'. The extra character 'd' is nonsensical in this context and disrupts the flow of the sentence.

comments.title: Grammatical error in table label definition
comments.quote: Comb represent the Com3 in the previous Section4
comments.explanation: The sentence uses the singular verb 'represent' with the plural subject 'Comb'. It should read 'Comb3 represents' or 'Comb represents the Com3 strategy'. This is a minor grammatical inconsistency that could stem from OCR processing of the original manuscript.

comments.title: Notation inconsistency between $M$ and $M^*$
comments.quote: The defense detection uses its negative proposition B: that is, there are manifold M* and algorithm A*. If the distribution of x is not P(X), then x is not on M*.
comments.explanation: In the same paragraph, the text references Proposition A (which establishes the manifold $M$) and then Proposition B (which introduces a new symbol $M^*$ without explicit definition in this excerpt). While the intention is likely to define the manifold of the transformed or processed space, the abrupt introduction of $M^*$ creates ambiguity. If read as a typo for $M$, it implies adversarial samples are 'off the manifold,' which contradicts the standard view that adversarial examples exist in the same high-dimensional space as benign samples, though they may cross the decision boundary.

comments.title: Ambiguous phrasing for Random Zoom parameters
comments.quote: where the distance from each edge of the image is cropped by U[10, 50], independently for each edge.
comments.explanation: The phrase 'distance from each edge... is cropped by U[10, 50]' is linguistically awkward. In image processing, one usually 'crops by' an amount (e.g., crop by 10 pixels) or 'sets the margin to' a value. Reading this literally suggests the *distance* is the object being cropped, which is physically impossible. It is much more likely the authors meant 'cropping margins of size U[10, 50] from each edge,' but the current phrasing could confuse a reader regarding whether the U[10, 50] refers to the margin size or the crop extent (which is the same, but the 'distance' word muddles the logic).

comments.title: Ambiguous structure of Proposition A
comments.quote: We can assume that the manifold is proposition A: then there is manifold M and algorithm A, if x is distributed as P(X), then x is on M.
comments.explanation: The sentence structure 'Manifold is proposition A: then there is manifold M' is grammatically broken and logically circular. It appears to be a transcription error or a poorly phrased hypothesis. The content seems to say: 'Proposition A states: If x is distributed as P(X), then x is on manifold M.' While the mathematical content (valid data lives on the manifold) is consistent with the Manifold Hypothesis, the formatting of the proposition statement is insufficiently clear to a reviewer.
